title: Beyond Commutativity: Redesigning Trotter Decomposition via Local Symmetry
abstract: The product formula, commonly known as Trotter decomposition, is a central tool for digital quantum simulation, whose performance depends critically on how the Hamiltonian is partitioned into tractable blocks. Standard decompositions typically rely on direct commutativity among Hamiltonian terms in a chosen operator representation, which can lead to large residual errors and deep circuits for complex, practically relevant many-body quantum systems. We address this fundamental bottleneck by introducing a new decomposition principle that goes beyond commutativity, grouping Hamiltonian terms into local three-site clusters according to the underlying SU(2) symmetry of the local dynamics. We show that three-site generators fall into at most four SU(2)-symmetry classes, each admitting an effective two-qubit SU(4) representation with exact and efficient implementations. By reducing the number of clusters, this decomposition principle substantially suppresses commutator-induced errors and circuit overhead while preserving underlying physical structures that commutativity-based decompositions may violate. We demonstrate the proposed method on several physically relevant spin-lattice models, where the reduced cluster structure can even realise the second-order product formula without doubling the circuit depth, as would be required by conventional decompositions. Numerical simulations of a Kagome Heisenberg model with triangular spin-chirality interactions show that the proposed method reduces both state infidelity and average spin-chirality bias by more than three orders of magnitude compared with conventional decompositions, while using substantially fewer gates. These results establish local symmetry as a flexible and practical design principle for product-formula simulation, opening a route to more accurate and hardware-efficient simulations of broader classes of many-body systems.
summary: This paper proposes a novel method to improve quantum simulation accuracy by redesigning Trotter decomposition based on local SU(2) symmetries rather than just Hamiltonian commutativity. By grouping terms into symmetry-respecting local clusters, the method drastically reduces residual errors and circuit depth for models like the Kagome Heisenberg lattice. This establishes local symmetry as a practical and powerful design principle for simulating complex many-body quantum systems.
main_problem: The primary challenge is improving the accuracy and efficiency of digital quantum simulation by redesigning the Trotter decomposition method.
main_result: The authors introduce a symmetry-guided decomposition that significantly suppresses commutator-induced errors and reduces circuit depth compared to conventional methods.
method: The method exploits the underlying local SU(2) symmetry of Hamiltonian terms, grouping them into local three-site clusters rather than relying solely on direct Pauli commutativity.
model_or_system: The work is demonstrated on several physically relevant spin-lattice models, including the Kagome Heisenberg model with triangular spin-chirality interactions and the 1D transverse-field Ising model.
key_observables: State infidelity, average spin-chirality bias, and the number of required quantum gates (e.g., CNOT gates).
important_parameters: The simulation time $t$ and the number of Trotter steps used for approximation.
main_assumptions: The core assumption is that the Hamiltonian structure can be effectively decomposed based on local SU(2) symmetry, which is superior to general commutativity assumptions.
figures_summary: Figures schematically illustrate the decomposition strategies on lattices (Kagome, 1D), comparing the number of required clusters (e.g., 10 vs. 2) and showing error/fidelity comparisons.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from identifying the limitations of standard Trotter decomposition to proposing the symmetry-based decomposition principle. It then applies this principle to specific models (Heisenberg, Ising) to derive concrete, error-suppressing product formulas.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, systematic design principle for Hamiltonian simulation that moves beyond simple algebraic commutation rules, directly impacting the feasibility of simulating complex, strongly correlated quantum materials on near-term quantum hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of an SU(2)-respecting trian- gular decomposition on a Kagome lattice. The Hamiltonian is partitioned into local three-site components supported on tri- angular plaquettes, shown as upward red and downward blue triangles, whose associated time-evolution operators preserve the underlying local SU(2) symmetry among the three ver- tices of each triangle structure.

FIG. 2. Algebraic structure of the generator space of three- qubit SU(8) operators. The orange region C denotes the com- mon three-dimensional Abelian generator space shared by the effective two-qubit sectors Hl. Each light-blue circular region Gl denotes the generator space of a local SU(2) symmetry class.

FIG. 3. Quantum circuit of the local propagator e−iHl∆t im- plemented by Eq. (4). The encoded evolution by Ul factorises into a single-qubit evolution exp(−iG′ l∆t) and an effective two-qubit evolution exp(−iH′ l∆t).
FIG. 4. Circuit implementation of the local propagator exp(−iH2∆t) for the local SU(2) symmetry class l = 2. The red dashed block denotes the encoder U2 by three CNOT gates.

FIG. 5. The connectivity graph of the 12-qubit Kagome XXX Heisenberg lattice with spin-chirality interactions within the triangular parts. (a) Clustering by the conven- tional decomposition. The two-qubit interactions ⃗σi · ⃗σj cor- responding to the edges are partitioned into four clusters coloured in red, blue, green, and yellow. The spin-chirality in- teractions ⃗σi·(⃗σj×⃗σk) within each triangle are partitioned into two clusters, coloured pink and light blue. In total, the con- ventional method partitions the interactions into the product formula with 4 + 3 × 2 = 10 clusters. Each pink or light-blue part is further decomposed into three clusters.
